coredata.dc:title: Low-cost devices with fluorescence spots brightness and size dual-mode readout for the rapid detection of Cr(VI) based on smartphones
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lizhi
Wang, Wei
Wang, Yuanhang
Li, Hongli
Zhao, Lei
Wang, Naiyu
Wang, Yan
Wang, Xiayan
Pu, Qiaosheng
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive, convenient and rapid detection devices for toxic Cr(VI) suitable for filed use are required. Smartphone can be used as the detector, but the quality of images taken with a smartphone may depend on the ambient light and the operator. In this work, two types of low-cost and portable smartphone-based devices used for fluorescence spots brightness and size dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) were constructed with the aid of the 3D printing, which avoids the effect of ambient light and maintains a fixed position of the phone camera relative to the samples. Based on the brightness reflected by the blue channel of RGB values of the images of carbon nanodots, a linear relationship between quenching efficiency and concentration of Cr(VI) in a range of 0.2–150 μM with a limit of detection of 0.058 μM was attained, which is comparable to or better than that from fluorescence spectrometers. With the size variation of fluorescence spots, a linear range of 10–350 μM was acquired and it is more intuitive for direct naked-eye estimation of the concentration of Cr(VI). The applicability of the proposed devices for the detection of Cr(VI) was verified with water and soil samples with recoveries ranging in 95.0–108.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone-based devices
Cr(VI) sensing
Dual-mode sensing
Carbon nanodots
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical property of CNDs and response of Cr(VI)
Selection of camera parameters for smartphone-based devices
Dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) on smartphone-based devices
Mechanism, selectivity and comparison with fluorescence spectrometry
Detection of Cr(VI) in practical samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design of the low-cost fluorescence analyzers based on smartphone that can be fabricated using 3D printing. (a, d) The 3D schematic of the fluorescence analyzers. (b, e) Enlarged versions of detection cell and UV LED. (c, f) Photographs of the actual smartphone-based fluorescence analyzers. The screens of the smartphones display typical images of blue-emitting CNDs sample when excited by a UV LED.
(a) The emission wavelength of UV LED. (b) The RGB value of the light with different wavelengths measured by the smartphone camera.

Effects of camera parameters on signal to noise ratio (SNR). The SNR was calculated with (a, b) red, (c, d) green, and (e, f) blue channel. (a, c, e) For the first row, the white balance (WB) was set to sunlight mode. (b, d, f) For the second row, the WB was fluorescent lamp mode. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Effect of illumination time on emission of CNDs and the mixture with Cr(VI).

(a) The images of CNDs with different concentrations of Cr(VI) captured by an OPPO smartphone. (b) The relationship between (S0-S)/S0 and Cr(VI) concentration in a range of 0.2–500 μM, inset figure is the linear relationship in the range of 0.2–150 μM. (c) The dependency of the length in pixel on Cr(VI) concentration. gv represents gray value, L is the distance used for the quantitative detection. (n = 3).
The images captured by smartphone device with centrifuge tubes as reservoirs.

Selectivity towards (a) cation and (b) anion interferences. Number 1–17 in (a) stand for blank, Cr(VI), Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, and Fe3+, respectively. Number 1–17 in (b) stand for blank, Cr(VI), Cl-, F-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, P2O7 4-, HCO3 -, CO3 2-, CH3COO-, HCOO-, ClO3 -, Br-, and ClO4 -, respectively. The top images were taken with the presence of corresponding ions.
The correlations between fluorescence method and (a) OPPO, (b) Xiaomi, (c) vivo, and (d) HUAWEI smartphone-based methods.
The dual-mode detection performance on smartphones of different brands.
Detection of Cr(VI) in environmental samples based on smartphone-based device (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Low-cost devices with fluorescence spots brightness and size dual-mode readout for the rapid detection of Cr(VI) based on smartphones

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects, Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Biology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects, Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Biology, Beijing University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects, Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Biology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive, convenient and rapid detection devices for toxic Cr(VI) suitable for filed use are required. Smartphone can be used as the detector, but the quality of images taken with a smartphone may depend on the ambient light and the operator. In this work, two types of low-cost and portable smartphone-based devices used for fluorescence spots brightness and size dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) were constructed with the aid of the 3D printing, which avoids the effect of ambient light and maintains a fixed position of the phone camera relative to the samples. Based on the brightness reflected by the blue channel of RGB values of the images of carbon nanodots, a linear relationship between quenching efficiency and concentration of Cr(VI) in a range of 0.2–150 μM with a limit of detection of 0.058 μM was attained, which is comparable to or better than that from fluorescence spectrometers. With the size variation of fluorescence spots, a linear range of 10–350 μM was acquired and it is more intuitive for direct naked-eye estimation of the concentration of Cr(VI). The applicability of the proposed devices for the detection of Cr(VI) was verified with water and soil samples with recoveries ranging in 95.0–108.2%.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) has been extensively used in various industries including electroplating, leather tanning, and wood preservation (Kieber et al., 2002). Cr(VI) is non-biodegradable (Deep et al., 2014) and considered as a serious Class I carcinogen to human beings (Wei et al., 2016). Cr(VI) released into water and soil may be transferred to human beings and leads to impairments such as oxidative stress, DNA damage, apoptosis, and changes in gene expression (Bagchi et al., 2002). Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a reliable and sensitive method for rapid detection of toxic Cr(VI). Currently, many analytical methods have been developed for analysis of Cr(VI), such as atomic spectrometry (Zhan et al., 2019), colorimetry (Swaidan et al., 2019), fluorescence spectrometry (Yin et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2016), electrochemistry (Tu et al., 2018), chromatography (Miyake et al., 2017), surface plasmon field-enhanced resonance light scattering (Han et al., 2007), capillary electrophoresis-inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Chen et al., 2015) and laser-induced breakdown spectrometry (Peng et al., 2017). However, these methods normally require expensive commercial instruments and skilled professional operators, which limit their wide use in resource-limited areas. More convenient and portable detection platforms are needed for the rapid detection of toxic Cr(VI).

Fortunately, the ubiquitous smartphones provide a chance for fabrication of cost-efficient devices for portable use. The smartphone is a multifunctional tool with high-speed computing capability and a high-quality camera (Wang et al., 2017). Recently, smartphone-based devices have been applied as detectors or as instrumental interfaces, some smartphone-assisted instruments are even commercially available (Roda et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2018). Among them, to use a smartphone as a detector has attracted much attention. Detection principles based on absorbance (Cheng et al., 2017; Guler et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2019), chemiluminescence (Gao et al., 2017) and fluorescence (Sun et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019; Han et al., 2020; Maruthupandi et al., 2020) have been used to design smartphone-based sensing tools. With the aid of smartphones, paper-based platforms were developed for convenient detection of virus (Huang et al., 2017), environmental pollutants (Guzman et al., 2018; Incel et al., 2017; Lian et al., 2017) and biomarkers (Rong et al., 2018). Smartphone-based portable fluorescence microscopes were developed for single molecule imaging (Khatua and Orrit, 2013; Wei et al., 2014), spores (Snow et al., 2019) and virus counting (Minagawa et al., 2019), and pathogenic bacteria identification (Müller et al., 2018).

Replacement of bulky and relatively expensive fluorescence spectrometer with portable and low-cost smartphone-based device is feasible, because 1) widely available ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV LEDs) with small size and low cost can replace xenon lamp as excitation source to enable fluorescent material emitting; 2) the smartphone camera can rapidly capture images, which are used for visual quantitation of analytes by color change; 3) burgeoning 3D-printed technique can flexibly print the holder for integration of smartphone and other necessary subassemblies. For instance, as described by Chen et al. (2018), a 3D-printed smartphone detector has been developed for detection of uranyl by capturing fluorescence signals on a test strip (Chen et al., 2018). Compared with the fluorescence spectrometer in lab, smartphone-based analytical devices are well accessible to non-professionals for on-site detection. Considering the importance of Cr(VI) detection and requirement of portable instruments, development of novel smartphone-based devices for Cr(VI) are needed. However, there are some limitations to use smartphone as the photodetector: 1) the image brightness may be seriously affected by the ambient light, so the measured absorbance or fluorescence intensity from these images may not be accurate; 2) the distance between the phone camera and sample may be hard to maintain same, and it also affects the accuracy of the measurement.

In this work, two cost-effective, portable and convenient smartphone-based sensing devices that can overcome the aforementioned limitations were constructed and used for dual-mode sensitive detection of Cr(VI). The devices mainly include a smartphone, a UV LED, power supply and other small parts. The camera parameters of the smartphone-based devices were optimized. The applicability of the smartphone devices for the detection of Cr(VI) was proved with three water and three soil samples. The designed smartphone devices possess the merits of high sensitivity, portability, simplicity and cost-effectiveness.

Guanidine hydrochloride was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,4-dioxane was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4) was bought from Lian long bohua Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Shuangshuang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Yantai, China). Except for Ag+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solutions prepared from their nitrate salts, other cationic solutions were prepared from their chloride salts. The solution of Cr(VI) was prepared from K2Cr2O7 (Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). Other solutions of anions were prepared from their sodium salts. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The filament used for 3D printing was black polylactic acid (PLA) plastic and purchased from Lanbo Printer Consumables Co., Ltd. (Cixi, China). Deionized (DI) water purified by the Milli-Q purification system (Merck Millipore, Bedford MA) was used in all experiments.

Triaminoguanidinium chloride (TGCl) was prepared with guanidine hydrochloride and N2H4 in the solvent of 1,4-dioxane as reported previously (Mitra et al., 2016). CNDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method. 0.14 g of TGCl and 1.26 g of CA were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water containing 1.0 g/100 mL NaOH under ultrasonication to obtain a homogeneous solution, and heated at 180 °C for 3 h in 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After it cooled naturally, a brown solution was obtained. The solution was centrifuged at 6559g for 15 min (one time) and filtered with 0.22 µm membranes twice for subsequent use.

Two smartphone-based devices were fabricated by integrating a smartphone (OPPO R11 Plus, Guangdong OPPO Mobile Telecommunications Co., Ltd., Dongguan, China) into accessories printed by JD073D 3D printer (Aurora Technology Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China). The 3D printing procedure includes: 1) The parts with defined dimensions were designed by the program provided with the printer in the STL format; 2) The STL format files were imported into the 3D printing program and set up the printing parameters such as infill, support and plate adhesion; 3) The files were saved as gcode formats and imported into the 3D printer; 4) Preheat the nozzle temperature to 200 °C and hot bed temperature to 50 °C and start printing. The main differences of the two devices lie in the solution reservoirs and the position of LED. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the first device was constructed to mimic laboratorial fluorescence spectrophotometer. The cavity of accessory fits the quartz cuvette well. The lateral hole in the right is equipped with a F5 UV LED (Fig. 1b). The LED intensity (reflected by power density) measured by a UV radiometer is 430 μW/cm2. The LED illuminated the sample from one side of the quartz cuvette. To weaken the background interference of UV light, the images were collected from a direction perpendicular to the excitation light. For the second device illustrated in Fig. 1d, 10 mL centrifuge tube was used as the reservoir. The LED is placed beneath the tube, which can illuminate the sample from the bottom of the tube (Fig. 1e). The two actual smartphone-based devices are exhibited in Fig. 1c and f. The construction of other brands of smartphones-based devices including HUAWEI nova 7 Pro (Huawei Technology Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China), Xiaomi MI 8 SE (Xiaomi Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) and vivo U3x (Vivo Mobile Communications Co., Ltd., Dongguan, China) was similar to the OPPO smartphone-based devices. The black PLA makes the 3D printed devices light-tight.

The UV light used in this experiment is low-powered and the designed device can avoid the direct irradiation of UV to the operator, but the protective measure should be taken if the LED is on and the phone is not attached to the devices. For the naked-eye observation, a piece of transparent polycarbonate sheet was placed on the position of the phone to filter UV light.

The CNDs solutions with different concentrations of Cr(VI) were placed in quartz cuvettes or centrifuge tubes. Typically, the cuvettes were placed into the cavity of smartphone-based device and the corresponding images were obtained. The images were processed with the software Image J to gain blue channel of RGB value for quantitation of Cr(VI) content. To test the applicability of smartphone device, three water samples and three soil samples were selected for the analysis of Cr(VI). The water samples were centrifuged at 10,248g for 20 min and subsequently filtered through 0.22 µm membranes. The soil samples were pretreated by a previously reported method (Pettine and Capri, 2005) with little modification for adapting to the pH used in this work. After the complete remove of humic substances by XAD-7 resin, pH was finally adjusted to 8.0. The detection procedure for all treated samples was the same as mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of CNDs and response of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
CNDs used for Cr(VI) detection were synthesized with a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme S1). The CNDs were selected because the materials have amine groups on their surface and suitable for the detection of Cr(VI) present in an anionic form. The 3D fluorescence spectrum of CNDs demonstrated that only one luminescence center was formed. Its optimal excitation wavelength was 340 nm, suitable to be excited with a UV LED, and the optimal emission was 410 nm, suitable to be detected with the camera of a smartphone (Fig. S1). Slight excitation wavelength-dependence of the emission was observed (Fig. S2). For the detection of Cr(VI), the larger the concentrations of Cr(VI), the higher the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Through standard fluorescence spectrometry, the determined LOD based on Eq. (1) (Swaidan et al., 2019) was 0.11 μM (Fig. S3). This sensing strategy for Cr(VI) was used to prove the utility of the smartphone-based devices.
(1)
LOD =
3 × standard deviation of the blank
slope of linear regression line

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of camera parameters for smartphone-based devices
ce:section.ce:para: The devices fabricated in this work solve two main problems: the effect of ambient light and uncertainty in the way of taking images, which may be encountered when a smartphone is used as a photodetector. The first problem was solved by the black shell of 3D printed devices and the second one was also eliminated due to the fixed position of the phone relative to the sample cell. As long as the camera parameters are fixed, images should be same for the same sample under an identical condition.

Considering the low cost and wide use of LED, 376 nm UV LED was chosen as the excitation source. The emission spectrum of LED is shown in Fig. 2a. Responses of the camera of the used smartphone (OPPO) on red (R), green (G) and blue (B) channels at different wavelengths were measured using Image J (Fig. 2b). Some overlap of the response can be observed, but its suitability for the fluorescence detection is evident. Approximate determination of the wavelength of an emission > 470 nm using the ratios of R, G and B values is also possible.

RGB color model provides independent values of color channels, they are important indices for color expression (Bueno et al., 2016). RGB values were frequently used to quantify the contents of analytes (Lian et al., 2017; Maruthupandi et al., 2020). To get a higher sensitivity, the effects of camera parameters including white balance (WB) category, shutter speed, and photosensibility (ISO) on signal to noise ratio (SNR) were evaluated. SNR was calculated using R, G, and B channel values, respectively ( Fig. 3). When WB was either sunlight or fluorescent lamp modes, B channel gave the highest SNR (Fig. 3e and f). Different ISO and shutter speed both had significant effect on SNR. The best SNR was obtained with WB of sunlight, ISO of 800, and shutter speed of 4 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) on smartphone-based devices
ce:section.ce:para: To test the stability of CNDs in the smartphone-based devices, the influence of continuous illumination of UV LED on CNDs and CNDs-Cr(VI) mixture solution was investigated ( Fig. 4). No evident change of the intensity of the fluorescence of CNDs was observed after 180 min of continuous irradiation of the LED. CNDs-Cr(VI) mixture also kept stable, which guaranteed the repeatability in the subsequent measurement.

With the proposed device, dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) based on a) brightness and b) size of fluorescence spots was proposed. Under selected conditions, the images of CNDs with different concentrations of Cr(VI) were taken ( Fig. 5a). For brightness-based detection, a fixed region (marked with red dashed lines) was chosen for every image and B channel values were read out by Image J. The dependence of the quenching efficiency of B channel value on the concentrations of Cr(VI) was shown in Fig. 5b. Linear relationship between the quenching efficiency and concentration of Cr(VI) in the range of 0.2–150 μM was obtained. The linear equation is (S0-S)/S0 = 0.00381C – 0.00569, where S0 and S represent the B channel value of the image of CNDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. The LOD based on Eq. (1) (Swaidan et al., 2019) was calculated to be 0.058 μM, which is comparable to or better than that attained by fluorescence spectrometry. In addition, with a proper program, it is possible to perform the data analysis on the same smartphone (Chen et al., 2018). The analytical performance of the smartphone-based visual method was also compared with other methods for sensing Cr(VI). As shown in Table S1, the sensitivity of the smartphone-based sensor was compared with the previous reported works that using different detection techniques (Breslin et al., 2019; Bu et al., 2016; Sadeghi and Moghaddam, 2014; Swaidan et al., 2019; Zhan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020a, 2020b). Although the sensitivity is not as good as atomic absorption spectrometry (Zhan et al., 2019), the value obtained with the proposed device is better than the most fluorometric and colorimetric methods. Compared with other smartphone-based sensors with single mode detection (color intensity) (Chen et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2021), the brightness and size of the fluorescence spots dual-mode detection of Cr(VI) provided more rapid and intuitive ways to monitor toxic Cr(VI) together with advantages of cost-effectiveness, simplicity, portability, and convenience.

For size-based detection, obvious decrease of optical path was observed along with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration. A quantitative approach was established using the size variation reflected by the length of dark side (L) of the optical path, as shown at the inset of Fig. 5c. The length (expressed as pixel here) was evaluated through the gray value obtained with Image J. The distance between the left edge of the cuvette (which is fixed for a given cuvette) and the point of 1/e of the highest gray value difference (inset schematic in Fig. 5c) was used for the quantitation. As shown in Fig. 5c, there was a linear relationship between the length expressed in pixel and Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 10–350 µM. Compared with brightness-based detection mode, the size-based mode can be used for intuitive naked-eye evaluation of the concentration. A wider linear range was acquired. It needs to be mentioned that the first device also can be used for naked-eye visual evaluation of Cr(VI), but the accuracy is not as good as the second one due to its shorter optical path.

Due to the difference in color balancing functions between different type of smartphones (Huang et al., 2018), three other brands of smartphones including Xiaomi MI 8 SE, vivo U3x and HUAWEI nova 7 Pro were also used to evaluate the dual-mode sensing system for Cr(VI). As shown in Figs. S4–6, similar performance was obtained for all smartphones. The results were summarized in Table 1. Although there are slight differences in the performance of different brands of smartphones, the sensitivities obtained with all tested smartphones meet the requirement for Cr(VI) detection regulated by WHO (WHO, 2011).

For the second device, 10 mL plastic centrifuge tubes were used as containers which allow the light pass through. LED was placed in the very bottom of tube. The CNDs solution was excited along the tube length direction. Moreover, the length of tubes is higher than that of quartz cuvette, which is convenient to observe the optical path. As shown in Fig. 6, with the perceived color variations, the concentration observed by naked eye could be low to 3 µM. Compared with paper-based platform, this device could avoid lengthy fabrication process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism, selectivity and comparison with fluorescence spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of CNDs for Cr(VI) was mainly inner filter effect (IFE), there was also contribution from the static quenching (13%) (Zhao et al., 2021). The IFE detection mechanism was also confirmed with the captured images. Higher the concentrations of Cr(VI), shorter the optical path. This phenomenon was attributed to the IFE, because Cr(VI) can shield the excitation light and emission light simultaneously (Zhao et al., 2021).

The selectivity was evaluated with the first smartphone-based device. 15 cations including Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+ and 15 anions including Cl-, F-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, P2O7 4-, HCO3 -, CO3 2-, CH3COO-, HCOO-, ClO3 -, Br-, ClO4 - were chosen as possible co-existing ions. No change of brightness of images was observed by these ions (Top images in Fig. 7a and b). Furthermore, the images were read by Image J and either the B channel signal (Fig. S7) or the gray value (Fig. 7a and b) maintained almost unchanged, which indicated that all cations and anions had virtually no interferences for the detection of Cr(VI).

The correlation between the responses of fluorescence spectrometry and smartphone-based method was evaluated. Linearities with correlation coefficients ≥ 0.97 were obtained ( Fig. 8). The consequence indicated that the performance of the portable device is comparable to that of standard fluorescence spectrometry. And by this good correction with fluorescence spectrometry, the detection of Cr(VI) with smartphone-based methods is possible and reliable to be used for the analysis in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of portable smartphone-based detector, six environmental samples including a tap water, a Yellow River water, a lake water, a garden soil, a river sediment and a street dust were analyzed. The images in column 4 are the images obtained with these samples, the effect of Cr(VI) concentrations on the fluorescence spot brightness and size is evident (due to the quenching, higher the Cr(VI) concentration, lower the brightness and smaller the spot size). It was found that Cr(VI) was lower than LOD for all these samples, but the spiked Cr(VI) at different levels were successfully determined with recoveries ranging in 95.0–108.2% ( Table 2). The results demonstrated that the smartphone device is reliable to detect Cr(VI) in environmental samples.

Because of the wide availability and good performance of smartphones, the combination of smartphone with portable devices can be a promising way for field use. Two kinds of inexpensive and portable 3D printed devices can effectively eliminate the problems from the ambient light and difference in images taken by different operators. With the first device, the LOD of 0.058 μM was obtained with brightness change (for OPPO smartphone) and that is comparable to that from the bench-top fluorimeters. Different brands of smartphones gave similar sensitivities. The size-based method can be used for intuitive evaluation of Cr(VI) concentration to 10 μM. With the second device, a lower concentration of 3 μM of Cr(VI) could be sensed by naked eyes. The practicability of the proposed device was proved with environmental samples with standard addition recoveries of 95.0–108.2% and RSDs < 4.9%. These smartphone-based devices show the advantages of simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and portability, and can be used by non-professionals for on-site contamination evaluation. It is also possible to use them for the detection of other pollutants based on the fluorescence either in “turn-off” or “turn-on” modes.

Lizhi Zhao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Validation. Wei Wang: Resources, Validation. Yuanhang Wang: Software, Validation. Hongli Li: Validation. Lei Zhao: Validation. Naiyu Wang: Validation. Yan Wang: Validation. Xiayan Wang: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Qiaosheng Pu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974057, 21625501, and 21527808) and the Beijing Outstanding Young Scientists Program (No. BJJWZYJH01201910005017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125986.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
